item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …. I have severe Prosopagnosia, known colloquially as Face-Blindness, my case is severe enough that I cannot recognise immediate family members or even myself. I wonder if this scip would have any effect on me, or do you have to be able to recognise faces to be affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. AoE cognitohazardous effect that affixes itself permanently to unfortunate victims. Think it should be Euclid though.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, it's well written and unsettling.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I'm in the minority here, the timeline actually kind of made me like the article a little less? I think toned back to Euclid, and stripped of the more 'human' actions and motivations, it'd be a lot more effective. 'Sentient location that is deliberately fucking with people" isn't scary in and of itself, and in going for it, you lose that uncanny, alien undercurrent that the whole article was trying to convey.

Still gonna upvote, because the SCP itself is great, just wanted to say that I'm doing so in spite of the timeline, not because of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. The treatment of the SCP-2551-1s is perfect: deeply sad and perfectly clinical. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. Very intriguing and genuinely unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I like the concept, I like the characterization of the Foundation, I like the unorthodox way it encourages the Foundation to "feed" it instead of just walling it off. But I do have a question.

██/██/██: Containment lost. Effect lost after approximately 12 hours of SCP-2551 being empty.

With no one inside to be susceptible to the effect, how did they know it was gone?

(Note: you don't have to answer this. What if they didn't know? What if their initial conclusion upon discovering a new area of effect was "it's spreading," and they had to do more work to figure out that containing the first location was no longer necessary?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you all for your feedback, and especially to user Jacob Conwell for offering advice on the first draft (even if I didn't take it all). Though not without room for improvement, I'll take this as a modest success for a first article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I'm reminded of fae abducting people and leaving changelings as imperfect replacements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the Incident Log and Footnote 1 don't seem to add anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this when it was in the draft stages on the forums.

Like I said then, I dig the concept well enough, and like the idea of changing conditions for the treatment of 2551-1 instances with each Site Director.

I still feel like you are going way overboard with the blackboxing in the timeline (seriously, what good is a timeline without dates) but thats mainly a nitpick.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't do much for me, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will say that this article did trim extremely close to cliche for most of the article. It did have a nagging 'something isn't right' feeling here though…

The part at the end where it made everybody follow Site Director Blank implies something much more sinister going on. That, and the fact that it keeps moving if they try to evacuate the area implies it knows what's going on and is toying with the Foundation. I love it when SCPs fuck with the Foundation like that.

That was the part that turned my downvote into an upvote. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article does a consistent job of noodling around and restating points that the reader should already be familiar with, while simultaneously relying on common SCP tropes that are more than a little hackneyed at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading along, gradually getting more aware of the nature of the anomaly, then
"there are currently 79 instances of SCP-2551-1…"
and it was suddenly horrific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unsettling. You've done a good job making a complex, abstract effect understandable in the description. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mysterious, and very creepy- +1

It also helps that

misrecognition of passing individuals as acquaintances

happens to me all the damn time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, excellent. Terrifying in a slow, creeping sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the description of this SCP is confusing to me, but I am undecided as to if this is a bad thing yet or not. Part of it being confusing may add to the general creepiness and the "WTF Factor" I think I would like to see a change perhaps just on the part that says subjects are "unable to be identified as human" I am not even sure that is exactly how it was worded, but i remember thinking that the wording didn't quite make clear what the exact aspect of the effect was doing. By reading further I realize that it means that there is something just not right about the individuals, but there was just something in that one part I thought could be worded a bit, i don't know, smoother perhaps is the word I am looking for? It just seemed like there was a bumpy part there in an otherwise very well written piece. I really enjoyed this one though and definitely has my vote overall. Thank you for your work, like i said I rather enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is friggen awesome. I'd hoped the title was going to be "I've just seen a face". +1 anyways.
